character.name: Charlie Osei

appearance.brief: Medium height, sturdy build, dark brown skin, hair in box braids she re-does herself (badly, on purpose) every few weeks. Wears her older cousin's hand-me-down hoodies even in warm weather.
distinctiveFeatures: A chipped front tooth from a bike accident she tells three different stories about
Draws tiny doodles in the margins of every worksheet — mostly weird little monsters
Wears a friendship bracelet from her old school that's fraying but she won't take off

mannerisms: Taps a pencil against her knee when she's holding back what she wants to say
Laughs a beat too fast to smooth over awkward silences
Picks at the fraying bracelet when nervous
speechPatterns: Softens hard truths with 'I mean, maybe it's just me, but—'
Uses humor to defuse tension before it gets serious
Goes quiet and short-answered when she's actually upset, instead of loud
personality.emotionalTendencies: Charlie feels things intensely but processes them privately first — she'll crack a joke in the moment and cry about it later, alone. She over-monitors the emotional temperature of a room and will bend her own opinions to keep things calm, even when it costs her.

background.family: Moved three months ago from Cleveland after her mom, an ESL teacher, got a new job; dad's a night-shift nurse, often exhausted, loving but distracted. Charlie is the oldest of three and has always been the built-in mediator between her younger siblings' fights.
interests: basketball (bench player, plays for the team not the stardom)
true crime podcasts she's not supposed to listen to yet
struggles: saying what she actually thinks when it might upset someone
still doesn't have a real friend group at the new school
internal guilt that she's 'too agreeable,' a pushover
secrets: She already lied once this year to avoid a fight with a girl at her lunch table, and it's been eating at her since
She actually really likes Priya's intensity and wishes she had the guts to care that openly about something

conscious: Make it through the group project without drama
Find a real friend group before the year gets worse
Not get on anyone's bad side three weeks into a new school
unconscious: Needs to learn her voice matters even when it creates friction
Needs to trust that telling the truth won't destroy her relationships

voiceGuidelines.vocabularyLevel: Casual middle-school vernacular, some self-aware humor, occasional overly formal phrasing she picked up from her mom ('per se,' 'technically') that she uses ironically
voiceGuidelines.sentenceComplexity: Short, quick sentences when nervous; rambles a little when trying to talk herself into something
voiceGuidelines.emotionalExpressiveness: Indirect at first — deflects with jokes — but capable of sudden bursts of honesty when pushed too far
examples: "Okay but hear me out — what if we just... don't panic yet? Panicking is, like, step four."
"I'm not mad. I'm just going to think extremely loudly about this for the rest of the day."
"Dez, I love your confidence, I really do, but that is not how bibliographies work."
"I keep telling myself it's not my job to fix it. Then I fix it anyway. It's a whole thing."

character.storyRole: Player character — the emotional barometer of the group, whose choices about honesty and confrontation directly shape how the project (and the friendships) survive.
arcPotential: Episode 'Group Work': Learns that keeping the peace by staying silent about Dez's shortcut isn't actually kindness — it's abandonment of Mo and Priya, and of her own integrity.
Future episode potential: Charlie starts a school newspaper/art zine and has to publish something true that upsets a friend, testing whether she's really internalized the lesson.
Future episode potential: Charlie's own group of friends forms partly from the fallout of 'Group Work,' giving her the first real belonging she's had since the move.

designNotes: Charlie is built specifically to make the episode's central dilemma feel personal rather than abstract. Because she's new to the school, she has an organic, high-stakes reason to avoid conflict — she doesn't have social capital yet and is terrified of spending it wrong. This makes Dez's shortcut, Priya's anxiety, and Mo's silence each land as a distinct pressure test: appeasing the charismatic kid, managing the anxious kid, and reaching out to the isolated kid all require Charlie to risk the exact thing she's afraid of losing — an easy, drama-free landing in a new place. Her defense mechanism (humor + agreeableness) is designed to be sympathetic, not annoying, and her arc isn't 'learn to be honest' as a moral lesson bolted on, but a natural extension of a wound she already carries (the guilt over an earlier lie). Keeping her gender/orientation unspecified beyond she/her and not centering her race as a plot point allows broad player identification while still grounding her in specific, textured details (braids, hoodie, bracelet, doodles) that make her feel like a real kid instead of a blank self-insert.
representationNotes: Charlie is a Black American girl from a working-class family, but her race isn't the source of her conflict — her story is about honesty and peer pressure, universal middle-school terrain, and she gets to just be a kid dealing with kid problems. Her family structure (nurse dad, ESL-teacher mom, two younger siblings) reflects a stable but stretched-thin household common to many players' real lives without being reduced to a 'struggling family' trope. Her conflict-avoidant personality is written with care to avoid the 'sassy Black best friend' stereotype — she's the lead, complex and internally conflicted, and her humor is a coping mechanism rather than comic relief for others.
